heading: MODULO SEDICESIMO
card_summary: Chunk 132 of De Harmonia Mundi presents "Modulo Sedicesimo," a theological treatise on Peace (Pax) in three concentus. Zorzi opens with Augustine's definition of peace as "tranquillitas ordinis" and elaborates a graduated hierarchy of peace from bodily health through rational-soul unity to divine concord. He argues that only God, as the cosmic Accordatore (tuner of instruments), can harmonize the human composite of body, soul, animal spirit, and rational will. Psalm 133 ("Ecce quam bonum") is expounded via Aaron's anointing oil and the dew of Hermon as Kabbalistic figures of divine influx, with the Hebrew word for dew, tal, decoded numerologically as the cognomen of the hidden divine Name. The chapter closes by tracing the ascent from peace to blessing, from blessing to life, and from life to union with the divine, which Zorzi promises to treat in the following section.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 132 constitutes the sixteenth *modulo* of the *Canticum Tertium*, eighth *Tono*, of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. Its subject is **Pax** — Peace — expounded in three numbered *concentus* that move from philosophical definition, through theological source, to contemplative praise, culminating in a Kabbalistic excursus on Psalm 133. The passage is a characteristic Zorzian synthesis: Augustinian moral psychology, Neoplatonic musical cosmology, Pauline pneumatology, liturgical typology, and Christian Kabbalah woven into a continuous argument about the harmonic constitution of the soul and its ascent to God.

---

## Concentus I: The Definition of Peace (§ 1)

Zorzi opens with an explicit appeal to **Augustine** (footnote 928), quoting the celebrated definition from *De Civitate Dei* XIX: peace is "la tranquillità dell'ordine" (*tranquillitas ordinis*). From this he derives a graduated taxonomy of peace that moves through increasingly spiritual registers:

- **Peace of the body**: the ordered composition of its parts, identified with *salute* (health).
- **Peace of the irrational soul**: an ordered, tranquil appetite.
- **Peace of body and soul together**: harmonious life and concord between the two.
- **Peace of the rational soul**: ordered knowledge and unified capacity for choice.

The highest earthly peace is the "felicissima riunione di due o tre" — the most blessed gathering of two or three — an allusion to Matthew 18:20, in which Christ promises to dwell among those assembled in his name. Zorzi reinterprets this gathering as the interior concord of the animal faculty with reason, reason with will, and will with reason. Out of this triple alignment arises authentic peace with God: the "pura osservanza della legge e della volontà che Dio ha manifestato."

The section closes with Christological flourishes: Christ as "Principe della pace," the angelic hymn of Luke 2:14 ("Pace in terra agli uomini di buona volontà"), and the city of celestial peace where God makes harmony reign — the last drawn from Job. The textual Latin interlude (lines 33–39) preserves the more technical formulation:

> *"dissonans (vel in minimo) ad harmonicum concentum, et canoram suavitatem admitti non debet, nec potest. Sic et qui minimo peccato, vel aliqua in parte voluptati inserviunt, non possunt devenire ad regnum, cum sint servi peccati, nec ad pacem illam, quae a quibusdam sapientibus diffinitur esse tranquilla animi libertas."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus I (p. 2540 ed. Campanini)</cite>

The image is unambiguous: entrance into the Kingdom of Peace requires a soul entirely free of dissonance — even the smallest sin disqualifies one from the harmonious choir. Peace is here defined by certain unspecified *sapientes* as "tranquilla animi libertas," the serene liberty of the soul.

---

## Concentus II: The Giver of Peace — God as Cosmic Tuner (§ 2)

The second *concentus* turns from definition to source. If no one can be free of sin without God, no one can enjoy or advance in peace without God. Zorzi invokes the liturgical formula "Da nobis illam, quam mundus dare non potest, pacem" (the prayer for peace drawn from John 14:27 and the Roman liturgy) to anchor the argument that peace is strictly a divine gift.

He then develops a **three-dwelling cosmology**: the *superna* (perfectly ordered and filled with peace), the *infima* (where Job's "sempiternus horror" reigns, with no order, rest, or peace), and the *media* (the human condition, a mixture of both). The middle dwelling presents the soul with a *bivium* — a fork in the road. Descent to the *inferna* is easy; ascent to the *superna* requires divine aid. The Virgilian echo of "facilimus est descensus Averni" (*Aeneid* VI.126) is folded into a theological reading: the ease of descent corresponds to moral dissolution, the difficulty of ascent to the need for grace.

The central metaphor of the passage then arrives in full development — God as **Accordatore**, the tuner of a musical instrument:

> *"sicut instrumentum indiget modulatore, qui fibras aut fistulas aptet, et consonas reddat, et postea debita sonoritate moduletur, sic quoque nos tamquam instrumenta (et utinam bona) indigemus modulatore, qui nostros aptet nervos et fistulas, qui procul dubio sunt anima, atque corpus, animal nostrum, et spiritus, superior portio, et inferior, voluntas, et ratio, et tandem omnia cum ipso Deo concordet."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus II (p. 2541 ed. Campanini)</cite>

The *nervos et fistulas* — strings and pipes — of this divine instrument are explicitly identified as soul, body, the animal faculty, the spirit, the superior and inferior portions of the soul, will, and reason. The image has deep roots in Neoplatonic musical theology (Boethius, *musica humana*) but Zorzi radicalizes it: God is not simply the composer of the cosmic harmony but the active, immediate tuner who must continuously maintain the consonance of the human composite.

Two Pauline texts are cited in support: Philippians 2:13 ("È Dio che suscita in noi il volere e l'agire") and 1 Corinthians 12:6 ("È Dio che opera tutto in tutti"). From God, therefore, flow:
- The peace and concord of soul with body;
- The subordination of the animal faculty to the spirit;
- The reciprocal correspondence of human minds;
- The correspondence of our mind with the divine mind.

Zorzi concludes: "Di là proviene l'accordatura degli strumenti, di là ogni azione sonora e armoniosa, di là viene la serenità, la pace, la soavità e ogni bene."

---

## Concentus III: The Sweetness of Peace and the Kabbalistic Excursus on Psalm 133 (§ 3)

The third *concentus* opens in a doxological register: certain *sapientes* (note 945) declare that nothing in nature surpasses peace in beauty and sweetness. Zorzi provides a eulogistic enumeration of peace's attributes — "serenità dell'anima, semplicità di cuore, vincolo d'amore, legame della carità, consolazione dello stato, gioia delle case, amica degli angeli, nemica dei demoni, gradita a Dio sopra ogni cosa." This catalogue has its antecedents in patristic and scholastic encomia of peace.

The central scriptural text is **Psalm 133 (132)**: "Ecce quam bonum et quam iucundum habitare fratres in unum." Zorzi identifies the "fratelli" — brothers — with multiple referents: soul and body in concord; all human beings together; a human being with his guardian angel. When this concord obtains, Christ (whose name in the *Canticle of Canticles* is "unguentum effusum," citing Song 1:3) diffuses himself over those assembled, spreading fragrance.

### The Anointing Oil: Aaron, Christ, and Priestly Mediation

The unction that "descends upon the beard of Aaron" (Ps 133:2) is read as the priestly mediation of divine *charismata*: the high-priestly unction flows from Christ (the head, *capo*) down through the ordained ministry to the people. The vestment's hem (*ora vestimenti*) to which the oil descends signifies the garment Christ "acquired and wove for us" to cover sin — a striking typological reading that binds together Christology, sacerdotalism, and the healing of the wounded soul.

### The Dew of Hermon: Kabbalistic Numerology of *Tal*

The second image of Psalm 133, the "rugiada dell'Hermon che scende sul monte di Sion" (v. 3), triggers the chapter's most distinctly Kabbalistic passage. Zorzi interprets:

- **Hermon** etymologically as "anatema della tristezza" (the interpretation found in Jerome's *Liber interpretationis hebraicorum nominum*);
- **Sion** as "specola" — watchtower — making the mountain a figure for those who govern and spiritually oversee others;
- **Tal** (טַל), the Hebrew word for dew, as bearing a hidden numerical significance:

> *"ros qui Hebraice (cid:684)ò tal dicitur, per viam numerorum cognomen importat magni nominis reconditi, a quo fluunt omnia."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus III (p. 2544 ed. Campanini)</cite>

The gloss on *tal* is a reference to **gematria**: the Hebrew letters ט (9) + ל (30) = 39, which equals the numerical value of יהוה אחד ("the Lord is one," from the *Shema*), a standard Kabbalistic equivalence. Zorzi thus encodes the dew of Hermon as a cipher for the divine Name (the Tetragrammaton) and its *cognomen* — a Kabbalistic technical term likely referring to the *Adonai* or a cognate title that serves as the exoteric complement to the hidden Name. The anointing oil figures the influx of the *Shem*, the Name proper; the dew figures the influx of the *cognomen*.

> *"per unguentum indicatur, quod habeamus influxum nominis, quod proprie est unguentum effusum, et per rorem influxum cognominis, adeo, ut nihil desit habitantibus simul in pace, et unitate."*
>
> <cite>Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, Canticum III, Tonus VIII, Concentus III (p. 2544 ed. Campanini)</cite>

The phrase "i teologi esoterici" (*secretiores theologi*) is Zorzi's habitual designation for Kabbalistic authorities. The movement is characteristically Zorzian: a literal/typological reading of a psalm verse is elevated into a sefirotic or onomastic register without abandoning the literal sense.

---

## Closing Ascent: Peace → Blessing → Life → Union

Zorzi draws the chapter to a close with a four-stage **eschatological ladder**:

1. **Peace** (*pax*) — the concordia of all the soul's faculties and of the soul with God.
2. **Blessing** (*benedictio*) — the gift through which beatitude is reached; those entering the Kingdom of Glory are called "benedicti" by the Prince (Matthew 25:34: "Venite benedicti Patris mei").
3. **Life** (*vita*) — eternal beatitude.
4. **Union** (*unio*) — the subject of the following section, which Zorzi promises to approach "nella misura del lecito e per quanto ce lo consentirà la debolezza del nostro modesto ingegno" — a rhetorical *captatio benevolentiae* that signals the approach of the work's mystical apex.

---

## Key Sources and Intertexts

| Source | Usage |
|---|---|
| Augustine, *De Civitate Dei* XIX | *Tranquillitas ordinis* as definition of peace |
| Matthew 18:20 | The gathering of two or three as interior psychic concord |
| Luke 2:14 | Angelic peace at the Nativity |
| John 15:5 | "Sine me nihil potestis facere" |
| Philippians 2:13; 1 Corinthians 12:6 | God as operative cause of all human willing |
| Psalm 133 (132) | Central scriptural text; oil, dew, brotherhood |
| Song of Songs 1:3 | Christ as *unguentum effusum* |
| Virgil, *Aeneid* VI.126 | *Facilis descensus Averni* |
| Job | Eternal horror in the lower dwelling; God's heavenly concord |
| Genesis 27 (Isaac's blessing) | Dew of heaven as divine benediction |
| Jerome, *Liber interpretationis* | Etymology of Hermon and Sion |
| Kabbalistic gematria | *Tal* (39) = *YHWH Ehad* (39) |
| Boethius / *musica humana* tradition | Human soul-body as musical instrument |

---

## Significance within *De Harmonia Mundi*

This modulo marks a pivotal transition in the *Canticum Tertium*. The earlier books treated cosmic, celestial, and natural harmony; here the locus of harmony shifts decisively to the moral and mystical interior. The musical metaphor, which throughout the work has operated cosmologically, is here fully internalized: the human person is the instrument, God is the tuner, and peace is the sounding of all faculties in perfect consonance. The Kabbalistic reading of *tal* is not ornamental but structural: it grounds the promise of Psalm 133 in the hidden architecture of the divine Name, ensuring that the peace celebrated by the Psalmist is not merely ethical but ontological — a real influx of the divine Name into those who dwell in unity. The chapter thus prepares the ground for the treatment of *unio* that follows, the summit toward which the entire harmonic edifice of the *De Harmonia Mundi* ascends.
